essential_functions: Critical Support Services (safety/life support systems)
Critical Operational Services (college-wide infrastructure disaster response/recovery)
Essential Operational Services (protect assets / support educational activities)
Important Services (normal college operations)

Areas of Critical Responsibility: Admissions & Records, Athletics, Auxiliary Services, Business/Finance, Career & Technical, Community Relations, eLearning, EH&S, Facilities, Health Science, HR, IT, Instructional Services, Campus Police, Residential Life, Student Affairs
rto_tiers: Four-tier targeted RTO / minimum RRO table: Critical Support = maintained at all times; Critical Operational = 1 day (RRO 3 days); Essential Operational = 3 days (RRO 3 weeks); Important = 3 weeks (RRO ~1 year)
succession_depth: Incident Commander succession by campus, ranked 'highest available person' (Fulton 3 deep, Tupelo 2 deep, Belden 2 deep); no broader org orders of succession
vital_records_approach: Not addressed as vital records; data center repair/IT recovery site covers data restoration but no vital-records identification/protection program
tte_cadence: Quarterly ERT meetings; >=1 EMC meeting/year; one tabletop exercise/year; building-evacuation drills/testing; annual ERT plan exercises; after-action Program Review
reconstitution_treatment: Recovery of Normal College Operations and gradual return to normal; College-wide Recovery steps executed if impact exceeds 72 hours; Crisis and Continuity Program Review revises documentation
academic_continuity_treatment: eLearning pivot: 'By utilizing eLearning and the Internet, maintaining ongoing academics should be possible under emergency situations even where multiple buildings are damaged, destroyed or if access to the campus is denied'; Instructional Services prepared to use eLearning; backup EOC at eLearning Center
it_continuity_treatment: IT maintains/restores intranet, data, video, voice, wireless; activates IT recovery site if severe; comprehensive damage assessment and repair/replacement of main data center; alternative emergency communications if cell towers down; appendix glossary covers redundant/hot/warm/cold site concepts
interdependencies_treatment: Mutual aid agreements with all state-supported higher-ed institutions and local high schools (athletics); outsourced food/water/bookstore providers required to have Business Continuity Plans; Appendix E Critical Suppliers and Contractors

unusual_decisions.decision: Frames recovery time tiers in business-survival terms tied to semester cancellation and institutional viability
unusual_decisions.quote: On the other hand, if this cannot be accomplished within three weeks or so, the College may be facing the cancellation of a semester and a refunding of tuitions.

unusual_decisions.decision: Requires all outsourced service providers to maintain their own continuity plans as a contracting condition
unusual_decisions.quote: Outsourcing – Food, water and bookstore operations are outsourced on an ongoing basis. All outsource providers are required to have “Business Continuity Plans” in place.

unusual_decisions.decision: Designates physical bunkering / pre-positioning mitigation (storm shutters, sandbags, sump pumps) ahead of forecastable hazards
unusual_decisions.quote: Bunkering – The College has storm shutters for most critical buildings that can be deployed in advance of a tornado. The College also has sandbags and sump pumps that can be deployed in advance of a flood.

analyst_notes: A 67-page institutional COOP integrated with a Crisis/Risk Management Plan and departmental Emergency Operations Plans, organized around six emergency-response priorities and a four-action-step recovery flow (Immediate Actions, Initial Recovery, College-wide Recovery, Program Review). Strongest on RTO/RRO tiering, recovery strategies, alternate/recovery sites, mutual aid and TT&E. Notably lacks formal FCD/NIMS doctrine citation, a true BIA, a hazard/vulnerability assessment, delegations of authority, vital-records management and devolution. Succession exists only for the Incident Commander role.

essential_functions: Curriculum Instruction (provide instruction for all programs; maintain Programs of Study; faculty contracts)
LRC/Library (research support; collection; circulation records)
Developmental Studies (tutoring)
Continuing Education (instruction/training)
Student Services: Veterans (VA-ONCE certification), Financial Aid, Registration & Admissions, Counseling
Administration: Payroll & Fringe, Cash Receipting, Accounts Receivable, Purchasing, Accounts Payable, Financial Reporting
Information Technology Systems
rto_tiers: Per-function 'Maximum amount time area could operate without function' (MTD-style): ranges from None (instruction, faculty contracts) to 1 hour or less (financial aid/registration), 1 day (cash receipting), 2 days (library collection/circulation), 30 days (veterans). No numeric RTO/RPO bands.
succession_depth: Not addressed (cross-training and 'backup' staff named per function, but no orders of succession)
vital_records_approach: IT backup procedures: daily full backups (Datatel via UFSdump on DLT tape autoloader; AD/Netware via Veritas); backup logs reviewed; tapes stored in a 2nd on-campus location (Grier Science Building) and removed by staff if computer room threatened; cloud-based survivability for Moodle/Gmail; off-campus system backups for payroll/AR
tte_cadence: No continuity-plan TT&E program; general institutional emergency procedures 'exercised...no less often than once a year' via safety committee
reconstitution_treatment: Explicit 'Return to Normal Operations (Reconstitution Phase)' in IT Continuity Plan and Disaster Recovery Plan: recovery activities terminated and normal operations restored/transferred back to MCC's computer operations facility
academic_continuity_treatment: Distance/online learning continuity: back-up Blackboard server or alternate distance-learning delivery; Moodle cloud-based; temporary instructional locations via use agreements with local school systems; cross-training to backup non-instructional functions
it_continuity_treatment: Full embedded IT Contingency Plan + Disaster Recovery Plan (pages 18-77): CONOPS, BIA, disaster risks/prevention, information-security policies, backup procedures, disaster notification list, damage assessment, alternate site (any wired building / off-site processing), return-to-normal/reconstitution
interdependencies_treatment: NCCCS System Office (NCCCSO) and mutual aid sites for compatible backups; common course library; payment services; off-site payroll/AR processing; cloud vendors (Gmail/Moodle); use agreements with Iredell/Statesville and Mooresville school systems; local healthcare agency equipment

unusual_decisions.decision: Directs IT staff to physically remove and personally safeguard the latest backup tape if the computer room is threatened, but never at personal risk
unusual_decisions.quote: In the event of a threat to the computer room facilities, administrative computer department personnel shall remove the latest backup tape from the computer room and maintain personal possession until a secure environment can be obtained. In no case shall personnel place themselves in undue personal jeopardy to obtain the tape from the computer room.

unusual_decisions.decision: Reserves use of an alternate site for catastrophic full-campus shutdown to the President's discretion
unusual_decisions.quote: In this event, select applications such as payroll could be performed by use of an alternate site at the discretion of the President.

unusual_decisions.decision: Built the entire plan on a 2004 NCCCS state mandate for a Financial Services Continuity Plan, then broadened it institution-wide
unusual_decisions.quote: The North Carolina Community College System (NCCCS) mandated in 2004 that each college should implement an appropriate Financial Services Continuity Plan to “ensure the timely delivery of critical functions and services to its stakeholders”.

analyst_notes: A 2011-2012 plan in two distinct halves: a department-by-department Risk Assessment / Business Continuity section (critical function, MTD, and facilities/personnel/technology threats-and-plans per unit) and a robust embedded IT Contingency + Disaster Recovery Plan with CONOPS, BIA, backup procedures and an explicit reconstitution phase. Driven by the NCCCS state mandate. Strong on essential functions, BIA, IT-DR, vital-data backup and reconstitution; weak on promulgation authority, formal maintenance cadence, succession, delegations, resilient continuity communications, devolution and a continuity-specific TT&E program. A 'Reviewed May 11, 2007' signature block exists but functions as a review record rather than a promulgation grant.

essential_functions: Sector 1: Information Systems/Business Technologies
Sector 2: Human Risk/Threats (campus safety)
Sector 3: Physical Plant/Facilities
Sector 4: Weather/Natural Disaster Risks
Sector 5: Campus Health/Environmental Risk
Sector 6: E-learning Service Interruption
succession_depth: Chief Public Information Officer (CPIO) role shared by the President and named proxies, one per building/area (Ron Layne COO, Dr. Rebecca Roush, Kellie Shoemake, Dr. Fallon Brewington, Germaine Elkins, Dr. Julie Voigt, Dr. Twana Mc...)
vital_records_approach: Not addressed (off-campus backup referenced only as a Campus-Level risk: 'off-campus backup failures')
tte_cadence: Emergency Response Procedures distributed annually via mandatory employee training; per-sector 'Periodic Campus Testing or Training' (e.g., annual network-closet/WiFi inspection; ongoing end-user training; ongoing review with Risk Assessment Team and Emergency Management Team)
devolution_treatment: Not addressed (CPIO proxies are a succession/role-sharing mechanism, not devolution to a separate unit/site)
reconstitution_treatment: Not addressed as a distinct phase; 'recovery' appears within each sector's Intervention and Recovery Strategy but no return-to-normal/reconstitution process
academic_continuity_treatment: Sector 6 E-learning Access Interruptions: faculty-based e-learning team maintains and monitors student access for virtual/hybrid courses; impact-level intervention and recovery for learning platforms
it_continuity_treatment: Sector 1 covers IT/Business interruptions with five impact levels (User/Classroom/Building/Campus/NCCCS-System), points-of-contact, ransomware/phishing emergency response, and the NCCCS Cyber Incident Response Call Tree (NC Joint Cyber Security Task Force, MCNC NOC); no formal DR plan with backups/alternate processing
interdependencies_treatment: NCCCS system-level dependencies (Colleague, IIPS, common course library, payment services); MCNC Network Operations Center; NC Joint Cyber Security Task Force; auxiliary campuses (Hoke County, Robbins Center, Larry Caddell Training Center)

unusual_decisions.decision: Uses a five-tier 'impact zone' escalation (User to NCCCS-system level) instead of time-based recovery objectives to drive intervention and formality
unusual_decisions.quote: The scale of impact determines whether Intervention and Recovery involves additional guidance and /or formal procedures.

unusual_decisions.decision: Distributes the Chief Public Information Officer authority across the President and building-assigned proxies
unusual_decisions.quote: A key figure in implementation of any portion of this plan is the Chief Public Information Officer (CPIO)—a title shared by the college President, Brenda Jackson, and her proxies in that role

unusual_decisions.decision: Embeds a real, dated cyber incident-response example (spoofed VoIP voicemail phishing) directly into the IT sector as operational guidance
unusual_decisions.quote: Yesterday the System Office was hit with a number of malicious emails containing malware.

analyst_notes: A color-coded, sector/risk-based Continuity/Crisis Management Plan (six sectors), reviewed annually as part of the Strategic Plan and Policies & Procedures Manual. It organizes around likelihood-ordered risk sectors rather than essential-function inventories or time-based RTOs, assigning intervention/recovery strategies, escalating impact levels and points-of-contact per sector. Strong on risk-sector coverage, communications/call-trees, cyber incident response, NCCCS interdependencies, and an e-learning academic-continuity sector. Lacks promulgation authority, BIA, RTOs, delegations, alternate-facility strategy, vital-records management, devolution and an explicit reconstitution phase.

essential_functions: Departments providing services essential to the mission (determined by the Business Continuity Steering Committee); each department lists its own essential functions/critical processes in Chico Ready
rto_tiers: Function impact/criticality ratings: 1 = Must Continue (Life, Health, Security); 2 = Must Continue in reduced mode; 3 = Pause if forced but must resume within 30 days; D = Deferrable; NA = Not applicable
tte_cadence: Per EO 1014: a portion of each plan tested every year, with the entire plan tested once every 7 years; Risk Management provides training to each identified department
academic_continuity_treatment: Not addressed (generic essential-services framing; no instructional continuity)
it_continuity_treatment: Not addressed (planning scenarios include operating without electricity/staffing/facility, but no IT disaster recovery)
interdependencies_treatment: Worksheet flags impact on Vendors and mitigation of loss of power/facility, but no dependency mapping

unusual_decisions.decision: Delivers the entire continuity capability through a commercial planning platform (Kuali/Chico Ready) with step-by-step department self-service
unusual_decisions.quote: The software program, Chico Ready, provides step by step instructions to assist each user in planning for continuity of operations without Electricity, Adequate Staffing or Facility/Building.

unusual_decisions.decision: Codifies a tiered testing obligation (annual partial / 7-year full) drawn from the CSU executive order
unusual_decisions.quote: EO 1014 requires that a portion of each plan is tested every year, with the entire plan being tested once every 7 years.

unusual_decisions.decision: Requires a physical signed cover page routed to Risk Management to formalize each departmental plan
unusual_decisions.quote: The Department head or designee will need to: 1. Print a copy of the plan 2. Sign and date the first page of the printed plan. 3. Forward a copy of the signed cover page to Risk Management.

analyst_notes: A short program-overview / how-to deck (22 slides) for CSU Chico's Business Continuity Program, framed entirely around CSU Executive Order 1014's seven program elements and executed through the Kuali 'Chico Ready' software. It is a programmatic framework, not an operational plan, so it covers governance well (EO 1014 authority, BIA/Risk worksheet with criticality tiers, departmental essential functions, training, plan review/renewal, and tiered testing) but omits operational COOP elements such as CONOPS, succession, delegations, alternate facilities, continuity communications, vital records, activation, devolution, reconstitution, IT-DR and academic continuity, which would reside in individual departmental plans.

essential_functions: Essential/critical campus departments identified via BIA as top priority to restore after a disaster (top-down campus-wide BCP with departmental BCPs as building blocks); critical functions defined as those whose loss within a specified time would cause unmanageable curtailment/suspension/termination of University operations
rto_tiers: Predetermined recovery time objectives referenced (BIA identifies priority of restoration and recovery requirements); activation flowchart uses 0-24 hrs and 0-48 hrs decision windows; no published numeric tier table in the master plan
succession_depth: President succession 9 positions deep via Presidential Directive 2009-03: President; Provost/VP Academic Affairs; VP Administration & Finance/CFO; VP Student Affairs; VP University Advancement; President's Chief of Staff; (for major disasters) Vice Provost for Academic Planning & Budgets; Chief of Police; VP IT/CIO
vital_records_approach: Records Retention Policy (BCP Team manages records per CSU policy and law; Records Retention Schedule); BCP manual stored offsite with offsite data backup storage media location, insurance contracts and means to reestablish physical records
tte_cadence: Section 4 Testing and Validation (with treatment of test failures); ongoing maintenance testing - oversight & remedial (ICQ 16): information update/testing, testing/validation of technical solutions, testing/verification of organization recovery procedures; Section 6 Business Continuity Training; plan re-evaluated after an exercise
devolution_treatment: Not addressed as devolution to a separate unit/site (succession + Emergency Relocation Site relocation cover related ground)
reconstitution_treatment: BCP Conditions of Termination: Emergency Director declares normal operations may resume when facilities/infrastructure/services are sustainable and reliable; activation flowchart includes 'Implement Reconstitution Plans' and 'Return to Normal Operations'
academic_continuity_treatment: First BCP Guiding Principle is Continuity of student learning: 'Ensure access to and consistent delivery of, academic education and related support services to students (existing and prospective) within the current and projected capacity of the University'
it_continuity_treatment: Disaster recovery infrastructure supporting work-process recovery within RTOs; testing and validation of technical solutions (virus definitions, technical deployments); offsite data backup storage; BCP manual includes technical requirements/readiness
interdependencies_treatment: BCP manual captures clients and vendors contact info, offsite data backup location, supply chain (means to establish a new supply chain); maintenance triggers include changes to important vendors/suppliers and their contact details

unusual_decisions.decision: Treats BCP as a phase distinct from crisis management, formally sequenced to begin only after campus stabilization
unusual_decisions.quote: Business Continuity: This phase involves restarting mission-critical (essential) business operations after a major disruption occurs.

unusual_decisions.decision: Hands BCP activation/operation authority to the Emergency Director rather than the BCP Coordinator, integrating BCP under emergency management command
unusual_decisions.quote: Upon the Emergency Director’s declaration of BCP activation, the BCP Coordinator coordinates BCP operation with the EOC Section Chiefs and the Emergency Management Recovery Team (Recovery Manager and Recovery Coordinators).

unusual_decisions.decision: Runs the program through a monthly Business Continuity Steering Committee that reviews, prioritizes, assigns and signs off on BCP tasks division-by-division
unusual_decisions.quote: BCSC is scheduled to meet once a month. These meetings are designed to be worksessions

analyst_notes: A genuinely comprehensive Business Continuity Planning Master Plan (v3.0) under CSU EO 1014, with a clear authority/delegation chain (VP Admin & Finance delegated by the President; Presidential Directive 2009-03 nine-deep succession), a full CONOPS (activation/operation/termination phases plus a COOP/ERS/reconstitution flowchart), three formal analyses (BIA, Risk/Vulnerabilities, Threat), a monthly Steering Committee, records retention, offsite backup, secondary work sites, testing/validation with treatment of failures, training, and a maintenance cycle. It satisfies all benchmark components except an explicit devolution of essential functions to a separate organizational unit. One of the strongest documents in the batch.

essential_functions: Organizational-level functions that must be continued throughout, or resumed as rapidly as possible after, a disruption; units determine priority of and perform unit essential functions in order of priority; Academic Continuity, Administrative Continuity and Research Continuity defined as preservation of academic core, administrative core and research capabilities
succession_depth: Unit order of succession required as a COOP goal; University President succession governed by a separate Delegation of Authority University Operating Procedure (referenced, not enumerated in the policy)
vital_records_approach: COOP goal to 'Establish means for identifying, backing-up and accessing unit vital records, files and databases'; Working Group includes Associate CIO, Information Security Officer and Director of IT Systems Architecture
tte_cadence: COOP Working Group develops and conducts Continuity of Operations training, exercises and continuous-improvement efforts; Emergency Operations Group Planning Team plans training/exercises and maintains the Integrated Preparedness Plan; emergency management teams train and conduct drills with Vermont Dept. of Public Safety partners
reconstitution_treatment: Recovery named as an emergency-management phase and Disaster Recovery defined ('quickly resume mission-essential functions'); Research Continuity references quickly reconstituting research resources; no dedicated institution-level reconstitution/return-to-normal process
academic_continuity_treatment: Academic Continuity defined ('preserving the functions of the academic core...including teaching and learning, scholarship, and research'); Registrar assigned Academic Continuity role; Research Continuity separately defined and addressed
it_continuity_treatment: Cyber Security Incident Response Team led by Information Security Officer; ISO 'ensures that a University system-wide, disaster recovery and incident response plan is in place'; IT/Security provides Cybersecurity/IT Support; Telecom and Network Services provide Communications/IT Support
interdependencies_treatment: IT, Procurement, Telecommunications and other support roles enumerated, but no explicit internal/external dependency, vendor, utility or mutual-aid mapping

unusual_decisions.decision: Defines three parallel continuity domains (academic, administrative, research) as first-class concepts in the policy
unusual_decisions.quote: Research Continuity: The capability to maintain critical research equipment, supplies, computers, unique data and specimens/samples, and records during disruptive events, or the capability to quickly reconstitute these resources and resume research after a disruptive event.

unusual_decisions.decision: Charters a standing, IT-heavy Continuity of Operations Working Group to produce the COOP, a simplified BIA and an all-hazard risk heat map
unusual_decisions.quote: produce a simplified business impact analysis; 3) produce and maintain a Continuity of Operations plan; 4) assist department Emergency Response Teams to develop unit continuity of operations plans; and 5) develop and conduct Continuity of Operations training, exercises and continuous improvement efforts.

unusual_decisions.decision: Acknowledges municipal/state authority can supersede University command and employ University assets in a larger response
unusual_decisions.quote: their emergency management functions may, to the extent of applicable law, supersede those of the University and employ University assets in the larger, coordinated response.

analyst_notes: An institutional Emergency Management and Continuity of Operations Policy (v3.25.1, approved by the President, with revision history) that integrates emergency management and COOP under NIMS/ICS and an all-hazards approach. It cleanly defines essential functions and three continuity domains (academic, administrative, research), charters a COOP Working Group to produce a simplified BIA and risk heat map, and lists COOP goals covering succession, delegation, alternate facilities, vital records and human capital. As a policy it stops short of numeric RTOs, dependency mapping, a budget/acquisition program, devolution, and a dedicated reconstitution process. Strong governance and definitional spine; operational detail is deferred to the COOP plan and unit plans it mandates.

essential_functions: Functions deemed critical to the continuity of UM business operations (evaluated by the Policy Group)
Departmental Business Continuity Plans identify critical functions and staff to maintain essential functions during emergency operations
Continuation of academic activities (Academic Impact Unit) and restoration of essential services
succession_depth: University President Succession of Authority (line of succession if President unavailable); Liaison Officer Succession of Authority; alternate staff designations for unavailable EOC positions
tte_cadence: Dedicated Training and Exercises section; EHS coordinates after-action reviews; EOP updated as informed by drills and exercises; CPRG reviews EOP annually
reconstitution_treatment: Recovery section plus Deactivation, Demobilization & Plan Reassessment; Business and Administrative Continuity restores administrative/business functions; Resumption of Academic Learning with Policy Group initial recovery planning
academic_continuity_treatment: Academic Impact Unit addresses academic impacts with the Business Continuity Branch; Resumption of Academic Learning: 'This may involve relocating students, conducting classes in alternative locations, implementing online learning and implementing temporary academic and financial procedures'
it_continuity_treatment: Not addressed as IT disaster recovery (CIO is on the EOP distribution list, but no system contingency/backup/alternate-processing content)
interdependencies_treatment: Heavy reliance on Missoula City and County for law enforcement/fire resources and statewide/regional response; Liaison Officer and Joint Information Center interface with external agencies; control of large incidents passes by statute to responding external agency

unusual_decisions.decision: Scales the University's role inversely to incident magnitude, ceding command to external agencies as scope grows
unusual_decisions.quote: The larger the emergency in terms of area affected, people at risk and magnitude of external response, the smaller relative role UM plays in the overall response.

unusual_decisions.decision: Stands up a dedicated Business Continuity Branch within the EOC Operations Section and a separate Academic Impact Unit
unusual_decisions.quote: The Academic Impact Unit will address needs, concerns and issues related to academic impacts resulting from the emergency in concert with the Business Continuity Branch under the Operations Section.

unusual_decisions.decision: Tasks the Policy Group to formally prepare a delegation of authority when mobilizing the incident management team
unusual_decisions.quote: Mobilize the incident mgt. team, prepare a delegation of authority, and be available to liaise with the Team on University priorities.

analyst_notes: A 2023-24 all-hazards Emergency Operations Plan built on NIMS/ICS with ESF-style functional annexes, signed via a Letter of Promulgation (President, AVP Campus Operations, EHS Director). Although primarily a response plan, it embeds meaningful continuity content: a Business Continuity Branch and Academic Impact Unit, President and Liaison Officer succession of authority, an incident delegation of authority, departmental BCPs identifying essential functions, a recovery section with deactivation/demobilization/reassessment, and resumption of academic learning (online/alternative locations). It lacks a continuity budget, BIA, RTOs, dedicated alternate continuity facilities, vital-records management, devolution and IT disaster recovery, reflecting its EOP (rather than COOP) center of gravity.

essential_functions: Critical functions identified and documented by each Unit and categorized by OEM-established levels of criticality (ranking/ordering standard)
rto_tiers: OEM establishes a standard for ranking/ordering critical functions by criticality; strategies for prioritizing, maintaining and/or resuming functions; no RTO/RPO/MTD times
tte_cadence: Each Unit conducts and documents at least one continuity exercise annually with after-action review and lessons learned (a real-world interruption may substitute); OEM conducts training for Continuity Plan Owners and Managers; Crisis Management Team participates in annual exercises
reconstitution_treatment: Recovery strategies/plans and 'resuming those functions' referenced in definitions, but no reconstitution/return-to-normal process
academic_continuity_treatment: Schools and research institutes are scoped as 'Units' that must plan, but no instructional-continuity provisions
it_continuity_treatment: Policy requires Units to develop, maintain and exercise business continuity AND disaster recovery plans; no IT-DR technical detail (set by OEM standard system)
interdependencies_treatment: Mitigation strategies for each critical function must consider 'loss of infrastructure, loss of personnel, loss of financial resources, and dependencies'

unusual_decisions.decision: Lets a documented real-world interruption with an after-action review substitute for the required annual continuity exercise
unusual_decisions.quote: In lieu of an exercise, Units may use real-word incidents that caused an interruption to the Unit’s critical functions. Units should still document the incident with an after-action review and incorporate appropriate lessons learned into their continuity plan.

unusual_decisions.decision: Centralizes a single criticality-ranking standard at OEM that every Unit must use, enforcing comparability across the institute
unusual_decisions.quote: OEM shall establish a standard for ranking/ordering critical functions based on criticality and all Units shall use this same standard when identifying their critical functions.

unusual_decisions.decision: Makes the Crisis Management Team responsible for forcing resource allocation to bring lapsed unit plans into compliance
unusual_decisions.quote: Ensures that Units with expired plans or with significant planning gaps prioritize the review of their plans and allocate appropriate resources to bring their plans into compliance.

analyst_notes: A concise governance policy (approved by the Policy Steering Committee, October 2023) that sets institute-wide requirements for unit-level business continuity and disaster recovery planning rather than operational procedures. It mandates identification/criticality-ranking of critical functions, mitigation strategies considering dependencies, designated Continuity Plan Managers, annual exercises with after-action reviews, and annual maintenance under OEM oversight, with leadership resourcing. As a requirements policy it omits a CONOPS, named doctrine, BIA, RTOs, risk assessment, succession, delegations, alternate facilities, continuity communications, vital records, activation triggers, devolution, reconstitution and academic continuity, all of which are pushed down to the unit plans the policy requires.

essential_functions: Mission-critical Information Resources and business functions identified in the Contingency Plan with associated contingency requirements; addresses maintaining essential mission and business functions despite disruption/compromise/failure
rto_tiers: Recovery objectives, restoration priorities and metrics provided in the Contingency Plan; recovery and reconstitution to a known state within the specified recovery time and recovery point objectives (RTO/RPO)
vital_records_approach: Information System Backup of system-level and critical user-level information plus system/security/privacy documentation; daily/weekly/monthly backups per system; daily backups copied to an offsite backup location at a geographically independent server room
tte_cadence: CIO tests, reassesses and maintains the Contingency Plan annually (virtual or simulated; an actual event satisfies the requirement); contingency training on assignment, on plan changes and periodically; lessons learned incorporated
reconstitution_treatment: Explicit 'Information System Recovery and Reconstitution' section: recovery and reconstitution of an Information System to a known state within specified RTO/RPO; eventual full Information System restoration without deterioration of controls
academic_continuity_treatment: Not addressed (IT/Information-System scope; instructional continuity resides in the separately-maintained TWU COOP)
it_continuity_treatment: Entire policy is IT/Information-System contingency planning: alternate storage site, alternate telecommunications services, alternate communications protocols, information system backup, recovery and reconstitution, resilience (load-balancing, off-site replication, monitoring), aligned to NIST SP 800-53 CP controls
interdependencies_treatment: Defines Contractors/Vendors, governmental-agency employees and TWU-Affiliated institutions in scope; alternate telecommunications via necessary agreements; coordinates contingency with incident handling

unusual_decisions.decision: Explicitly maps its provisions to a named, partially-adopted NIST control set, disclosing the gaps in the state's adoption
unusual_decisions.quote: The State of Texas has chosen to adopt a select number of Contingency Planning (“CP”) principles established in NIST SP 800-53 “Contingency Planning” guidelines... so there are gaps in the numbering sequence.

unusual_decisions.decision: Counts an actual contingency activation as fulfilling the annual test requirement
unusual_decisions.quote: If an actual event occurs that requires that the Contingency Plan be executed, that event fulfills the annual test requirement.

unusual_decisions.decision: Mandates geographically independent, access-controlled server rooms with equivalent controls at the alternate site
unusual_decisions.quote: ITS shall maintain geographically independent server rooms where data is stored in a secure, environmentally safe, locked facility accessible only to TWU representatives.

analyst_notes: A NIST-aligned Information System Contingency Planning regulation (URP 04.775) that is explicitly the IT/DR companion to TWU's separately-maintained Continuity of Operations Plan (held by Risk Management under URP 04.410). It cites NIST SP 800-53 CP-1 through CP-11, 1 TAC Ch. 202 and the Texas DIR Security Standards Catalog, and covers mission-critical functions, RTO/RPO, alternate storage and telecommunications sites, backups, recovery and reconstitution, training and annual testing. As an IT-DR policy it omits a CONOPS narrative, BIA (deferred to the COOP), threat assessment, succession, delegations, activation triggers, devolution and academic continuity. Strong, doctrine-grounded IT contingency coverage.

essential_functions: IT systems necessary to recover essential business functions or dependent business functions (each identified with RTO/RPO); identification of all critical data, applications, documentation, personnel, facilities and support items necessary during recovery
rto_tiers: Recovery time objectives (RTOs) and recovery point objectives (RPOs) established by System/Data Owners for each IT system, based on the BIA and Risk Assessment; alternate processing site resumption within an organization-defined time period consistent with RTO/RPO
vital_records_approach: Data Backup and Restoration plan with secure off-site storage of backup media; full/incremental backup scheme; periodic test restores; retention per State Library of Virginia records retention policies; e-discovery readiness
tte_cadence: Backup/recovery procedures tested at least annually (not to exceed one year between tests, documented); annual exercise (or more often) of the VSU COOP and IT DR components; review and revision of IT DR components following each exercise
reconstitution_treatment: Transfer and resumption of information system operations for essential missions/business functions at an alternate processing site; ensures IT services can be resumed following an event causing loss of data
academic_continuity_treatment: Not addressed (IT contingency scope; academic continuity resides in the broader VSU COOP)
it_continuity_treatment: Entire policy is IT contingency planning / disaster recovery: BIA-driven IT-system RTO/RPO, backup and recovery plan, off-site and alternate storage site with agreements, alternate processing site with equivalent security safeguards, test restores, incident notification, local/remote access communication methods
interdependencies_treatment: Recovers 'essential business functions or dependent business functions'; scope covers contractors, employees, vendors and student interns; alternate storage/processing sites require necessary agreements; business partners follow best practices

unusual_decisions.decision: Designates a dedicated IT 'TS COOP Coordinator' to collaborate with the university COOP coordinator as the focal point bridging IT-DR and institutional COOP
unusual_decisions.quote: The Technology Services (TS) Continuity of Operations (COOP) Coordinator is the designated IT employee responsible for collaboration with the university’s Continuity Plan (CP) Coordinator as the focal point for IT aspects of Continuity Plan and related Disaster Recovery (DR) planning activities.

unusual_decisions.decision: Requires the alternate storage and processing sites to provide information-security safeguards equivalent to the primary site
unusual_decisions.quote: ensures that the alternate storage site provides information security safeguards equivalent to that of the primary site.

unusual_decisions.decision: Ties electronic-records management explicitly to legal-discovery producibility and the State Library of Virginia retention schedule
unusual_decisions.quote: Retention of the data handled by an IT system must be in accordance with the State Library of Virginia records retention policies and procedures.

analyst_notes: A five-page IT Contingency Planning Policy (6135, approved by the President 9/6/17) structured around NIST 800-34/800-53-style contingency controls though without naming the doctrine in-text. It is explicitly the IT-DR component of the university's broader VSU COOP, assigning roles to System/Data Owners and a TS COOP Coordinator and requiring a BIA, IT-system RTOs/RPOs, backup/restoration with off-site and alternate storage, an alternate processing site, test restores, annual exercises and post-exercise revision. Promulgated by the President. As an IT-DR policy it omits a CONOPS, succession, delegations, activation triggers, devolution and academic continuity, which live in the parent COOP.

essential_functions: Critical district services and critical application servers (Banner System and interfacing third-party apps, e-mail, Internet, Intranet, file sharing, network authentication, DNS, DHCP, network management); critical computing capabilities to be restored in an orderly course of action
vital_records_approach: Banner servers backed up to tape on full/partial schedule with tapes rotated offsite weekly, plus backup to Amazon Web Services (AWS) cloud for double redundancy; 365-day minimum retention for sensitive data; data on servers/SANs in scope
tte_cadence: No DR plan-level training/exercise/after-action program (equipment-level checks only: generator tested monthly/weekly, fire-suppression annual testing, UPS self-tests, test/replica databases)
reconstitution_treatment: Documents disaster recovery/emergency activation, execution and reconstitution procedures; defines a reconstitution mechanism to get business back to normal from a disaster-recovery state; reconstitution relies on maintenance agreements for technical expertise and spare components
academic_continuity_treatment: Context only: notes students rely on ITS to register, conduct online instruction and communicate with faculty and that 24x7 availability became business-critical with COVID-19 remote work/learning; no instructional-continuity provisions (academic continuity is not the plan's purpose)
it_continuity_treatment: Full operational ITS Disaster Recovery Plan: IT/network infrastructure inventory across Chabot/Las Positas/EDCE/Dublin, hardware/software redundancy, backup systems, UPS/generator power and cooling resilience, fire suppression, two service-interruption levels (predictable vs. exceptional failures), DR initiation/detection, execution and reconstitution, plus detailed server/database recovery appendices
interdependencies_treatment: Dependencies on service providers to repair their infrastructure after an earthquake; maintenance agreements with hardware/software/infrastructure vendors for spare components and expertise; insurance carriers consulted on repair-vs-temporary-facilities decisions; Banner/third-party application interfaces

unusual_decisions.decision: Bounds the plan's ambition explicitly, declining to plan for district-wide catastrophe while still guiding central-computing restoration
unusual_decisions.quote: The Disaster Recovery plan is not designed to address conditions of widespread damage throughout the District.

unusual_decisions.decision: Pairs weekly offsite tape rotation with AWS cloud backup to achieve deliberate double redundancy for the Banner system
unusual_decisions.quote: In addition to the tape backup, the systems are backed up to Amazon Web Services (AWS) in the cloud. This provides for double redundancy in the even that a backup need to be access for file recovery.

unusual_decisions.decision: Routes the repair-versus-relocate decision through ITS management together with the District's insurance carriers
unusual_decisions.quote: ITS management and other District management would work with the District’s insurance carriers to determine whether to pursue repair or to secure temporary facilities.

analyst_notes: A 34-page operational ITS Disaster Recovery Plan (April 2021, submitted by the CTO) for a multi-college district, organized around response/recovery/resumption/restoration/return phases plus DR initiation and reconstitution. It is detailed on IT and facility infrastructure, redundancy (hardware, UPS/generator, cooling, fire suppression), offsite+AWS backups, threat/risk profiling (earthquake/fire/computer crime), alternate/temporary facilities and a reconstitution mechanism backed by vendor maintenance agreements. Because it is IT-DR scoped and authored as a submitted technical document, it lacks a promulgation/approval grant, named doctrine, a plan-maintenance cadence, BIA, RTO/RPO, succession, delegations, devolution, academic-continuity provisions and a DR-exercise/training program.

essential_functions: Department's functions essential to operational continuity and/or recovery, each with responsible position title, primary and alternate (cross-trained) personnel (Section F worksheet); university objectives include continuing functions essential to university operations
succession_depth: Leadership Succession (Section G): Head of Operations, First Successor, Second Successor, Third Successor (3 deep) who can make operational decisions if the head is absent
vital_records_approach: Section E Emergency Access to Information and Systems: identify critical data/records, whether backed up on-site or off-site (off-site data backup, backup files on flash drives, hard copies), and simulate a failure scenario that tests the ability to recover lost critical data
tte_cadence: Section K Exercising Your Plan: staff orientation meeting, call tree drill, tabletop exercise, interdepartmental exercise, emergency assembly drill, emergency communication test, off-site information access test, unscheduled work-at-home day, with scheduled dates and staff distribution date
reconstitution_treatment: Section L Recovery: plan to fully resume operations as soon as possible after the crisis, addressing resumption/scheduling of normal activities, work backlog, resupply of inventories, absenteeism and emotional needs
academic_continuity_treatment: University objective to 'Resume normal teaching, research and service operations as soon as possible'; departments classify principal nature of operations (Instruction, laboratory/other research, research support); departments state teaching/research/service objectives
it_continuity_treatment: Section E covers emergency access to information/systems: remote access (or authorization), contacting IT support, Blackboard, off-site data backup, backup files, hard copies, alternate email; includes a recovery-test of lost critical data
interdependencies_treatment: Section H Key Internal (within NMSU) Dependencies (ICT, Payroll, Purchasing, Business & Finance, Fire/Police, HR, Facilities & Services, plus department-listed) and Section I Key External Dependencies (suppliers/providers with primary/alternate, encouraged to prepare their own COOP)

unusual_decisions.decision: Embeds employee household/family preparedness into the institutional COOP on the premise that staff will not report if their families are unsafe
unusual_decisions.quote: Past experience has taught us that employees may not show up for work if they are concerned for the safety and security of their families.

unusual_decisions.decision: Builds a recoverability self-test directly into the template by asking departments to simulate a data-loss scenario
unusual_decisions.quote: Simulate a failure scenario that tests the ability to recover “lost” critical data.

unusual_decisions.decision: Encourages departments to push continuity planning out to their external suppliers
unusual_decisions.quote: We recommend that you encourage them to prepare continuity of operations plan.

analyst_notes: A six-page fillable All-Hazards COOP template for NMSU departments, structured around essential functions and recovery and submitted to a Dean/VP for approval. Despite its brevity it captures an unusually broad set of components: an all-hazards threat list, essential functions with primary/alternate cross-trained personnel, three-deep leadership succession, internal and external dependency inventories, emergency communications with backups, emergency access to systems (off-site data backup and a recovery self-test), mitigation strategies including telework, an exercise menu with dates, and a recovery section. It lacks named doctrine, a continuity budget, a maintenance cadence, a true BIA, RTOs, formal delegations of authority with triggers, explicit activation procedures and devolution.

essential_functions: Essential/Critical Functions per Federal Preparedness Circular 65 (Cal Maritime uses 'Critical Function' for Essential Function): provide vital/mission-critical services, exercise civil authority, maintain public safety, sustain industrial/economic base; mission-critical functions named include instruction to enrolled students, facility security and employee communication; Essential/Critical Function Summary and Detail worksheets
rto_tiers: Four-level criticality classification: Critical 1 = cannot pause, necessary to life/health/security; Critical 2 = continue if at all possible, reduced/alternative mode; Critical 3 = may pause but must resume within 30 days or sooner; Critical 4 = deferrable, resume when conditions permit (applied to both functions and IT systems)
succession_depth: Leadership successor captured for the unit head ('a person who would be an appropriate substitute if the head of the unit is absent'; explicitly noted as usually not an officially-designated position); Key Personnel and Alternates collected
vital_records_approach: 'Vital Records and Information' is a required field in the Essential/Critical Function Detail worksheet (resource requirements), alongside network requirements and facilities/equipment; planning prompts include 'What if network data is not available?'
tte_cadence: Per EO 1014: testing of some parts of the BCP annually and all parts every seven years to identify plan deficiencies; initial training to all individuals responsible for developing/implementing the plan, with additional training as necessary; a review log maintained
reconstitution_treatment: Stated as a goal ('respond to an event and return to performing its essential functions as quickly as possible') but no reconstitution/return-to-normal process; broader recovery referenced out to the Emergency Preparedness Guidelines
academic_continuity_treatment: Instruction to enrolled students named as a mission-critical function; classroom instruction classified Critical 3; work-from-home and remote connectivity action items for faculty/students when office/lab space is damaged; research addressed
it_continuity_treatment: IT system criticality is assessed per department (same four-tier scale), but IT disaster recovery is explicitly out of scope ('The disaster recovery plans for the University’s network and servers are outside the scope of this document')
interdependencies_treatment: Upstream and downstream dependencies captured in the Function Detail worksheet; framing questions 'who produces what we need?' and 'who needs what we produce?'; network requirements (campus network for e-mail/Internet) listed as a resource requirement

unusual_decisions.decision: Anchors essential-function definitions in Federal Preparedness Circular 65 while renaming them 'Critical Functions' for local use
unusual_decisions.quote: An Essential Function (as defined in the Federal Preparedness Circular 65) is a function that enables an organization to [Note: Cal Maritime uses the term Critical Function to describe Essential Functions]

unusual_decisions.decision: Explicitly carves IT network/server disaster recovery out of the departmental BCP scope
unusual_decisions.quote: The disaster recovery plans for the University’s network and servers are outside the scope of this document.

unusual_decisions.decision: Defines delegation of authority as a written instrument and prompts departments to treat action items as ideas rather than resource-bound commitments
unusual_decisions.quote: A formal delegation of authority is an assignment of authority and responsibility to perform specified acts on behalf of the organization. This assignment is almost always granted via a written document.

analyst_notes: A 14-page Department Business Continuity Plan template (v2.0) under CSU EO 1014, which uniquely cites Federal Preparedness Circular 65 for essential functions. It is well-structured around essential/critical functions with a four-tier criticality scale doubling as RTO bands, an effectively BIA-style function-detail worksheet (upstream/downstream dependencies, vital records, network/facility resources and 'what-if' impact prompts), leadership successor and key-personnel capture, a defined delegation of authority, telework/off-campus continuity, plan maintenance with a review log, and EO 1014 testing/training cadence. It is promulgated via versioned approval. It lacks a continuity budget, a systematic threat/hazard assessment, devolution, a reconstitution process, and IT disaster recovery (explicitly out of scope).
